does: This paper provides a theoretical analysis of what Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) extract from neural network representations by characterizing the properties that must hold at local optima of the SAE optimization problem. Rather than assuming a particular data-generating model, the authors extend local optimality analysis techniques to the nonnegative joint-optimization problem that SAEs solve, deriving constraints on the structure of learned dictionaries at convergence. The work establishes what properties make a feature or concept extractable by SAEs, grounding the empirical success of SAEs in interpretability work with formal theoretical guarantees about dictionary structure and feature recovery.
connects: This work builds on the extensive identifiability literature in sparse coding that studies when dictionary learning algorithms can recover ground-truth sparse features, including foundational analyses by Gribonval and Schnass on local optimality conditions. However, it departs from traditional approaches that rely on simplified data-generating assumptions like independent sparse features, which poorly match real language model representations. The paper connects to the recent wave of interpretability research using SAEs to decompose neural network activations into human-understandable concepts, while addressing a key theoretical gap: why SAEs succeed empirically despite operating on complex, high-dimensional learned representations rather than synthetic data.
enables: This theoretical framework enables future work to predict when and why SAEs will successfully extract interpretable features from specific neural representations, moving beyond purely empirical validation. The characterization of optimal dictionary structure could guide improved SAE architectures and training procedures by identifying which properties to encourage or enforce. The work opens directions for analyzing tradeoffs between interpretability and reconstruction quality, understanding failure modes when SAEs don't extract meaningful concepts, and potentially developing tighter bounds or new algorithms leveraging the optimality structure. This foundation could also enable formal analysis of whether SAE-discovered features are causally meaningful for model behavior.
